# EDDT Glossary

This glossary defines the vocabulary used across the data analytics ecosystem
design documents. Definitions are self-standing — each entry can be read
independently of its grouping. Groups exist for organisational convenience only.

---

## 1. Operational Pattern

### OODA Loop

The Observe–Orient–Decide–Act decision cycle that governs the system's
continuous operational pattern. The system observes the environment, structures
observations into a model, reasons about what action is warranted, and effects
change. The cycle repeats continuously.

### Observe

The first phase of the OODA loop. The system collects data from external
interfacing systems (via instruments) and from its own internal components (via
telemetry). Observation is inherently asynchronous and may yield partial,
overlapping, or stale coverage.

### Orient

The second phase of the OODA loop. Raw observations are structured into a
coherent, queryable model of reality through progressive data tiering and the
Event-Driven Digital Twin.

### Decide

The third phase of the OODA loop. The system applies analytical reasoning to the
oriented model — progressing from exploratory analysis through formalised
algorithmics, calibration, and real-time signal generation.

### Act

The fourth phase of the OODA loop. The system effects change — either through
automated governors that push changes to interfacing systems, or through
operational playbooks that guide human-mediated responses.

### System of Interest (SoI)

The entity being designed, developed, and operated. In this context, the SoI is
the entire platform: all deployments, all shared infrastructure, and the
software and data assets that comprise the framework. It is not a single
deployment instance; it is the aggregate of all deployed instances together
with the common platform from which they are derived. The SoI boundary
determines what is inside the system and what is external (interfacing,
enabling, or interoperating).

### Interfacing System

An external system that shares a data or information interface with the SoI but
lies outside its boundary. In this context, interfacing systems are primarily
the components of the operator's observed infrastructure — instrumented
devices, gateways, endpoint equipment, and management interfaces. The SoI
observes and, in some cases, acts upon these systems, but does not own or
control them.

### Enabling System

An external system that facilitates the lifecycle activities of the SoI but is
not part of its operational environment. Examples include CI/CD pipelines,
development tooling, test infrastructure, and engineering collaboration systems.
A failure in an enabling system can block development of the SoI even though the
enabling system is not visible to end users.

### Interoperating System

An external system that interfaces with the SoI within its operational
environment to perform a common function. Examples include partner analytics
platforms and operations centres that jointly participate in coordinated
detection and response workflows alongside the SoI.

### Concept of Operations (ConOps)

An organisational-level description of the enterprise's high-level operational
intent, strategies, and the overall environment in which an existing or future
capability will be used. The ConOps is system-agnostic — it describes the
problem space and operational context rather than a specific solution. It
outlines the current state, the desired future state, and the operational gaps
that need to be bridged. The ConOps provides the enterprise context from which
stakeholder needs are derived.

### Operational Concept (OpsCon)

A system-level description of how a specific proposed system will operate within
the broader environment defined by the ConOps to fulfil stakeholder needs. The
OpsCon is solution-specific — it defines how the system will be used, including
operational scenarios, user roles, system states and modes, and interactions
with its environment. The OpsCon serves as the bridge between stakeholder needs
and formal system requirements.

---

## 2. Event Processing

### Event

The fact that a state change occurred somewhere in the system. An event is an
immutable, atomic and logical occurrence — something happened. The observation
system may not always be able to fully observe the state change that occurred as
a result of the event.

### State

A property or mode of a stateful entity within its state machine — "where the
entity currently is" from the point of view of its defining behaviour. State is
bound to the entity, managed *in place*, and does not traverse system
interfaces. A state transition is a logical occurrence within the system — an
event. Knowledge about an event is carried by a notification, a representation
that encodes the information describing what occurred (including, but not
limited to, a delta describing the state change or where applicable, a more
complete representation of the state at an instant i.e. a snapshot).
Consistently with this framing, the runtime subject namespace has a single
data-plane element-type: only notifications flow on the wire; events are the
underlying logical occurrences that notifications describe; state itself remains
with the entity.

### Notification

A codified signal that represents knowledge about one or more events. A
notification describes what is known about a state change — when it occurred,
where it occurred, what changed, and to what extent. Unlike an event (which is
the bare fact that something happened), a notification is an intentional,
structured communication that may aggregate, filter, enrich, or transform
information derived from underlying events. Delta representations and snapshot
representations are specialised forms of notifications.

### Payload

The data content carried by a notification within a subject. The payload schema
is defined by the tier. At Bronze, payload schemas are dictated by the
instrument. At Silver and Gold, payload schemas conform to the system's
normalised data domain model.

### Signal

An automated output produced by the data processing pipeline in response to
patterns detected in Silver tier data. Signals may trigger governors (automated
action), inform operational playbooks (human-mediated action), or inform Gold
tier aggregations. Detection is scoped to Silver so that internal reasoning
operates on the validated, tight-latency substrate, leaving Gold as the
external- and user-facing aggregation layer with its own lifetime and SLA
envelope.

---

## 3. Data Architecture

### Event-Driven Digital Twin (EDDT)

The runtime architecture that continuously reconstructs a high-fidelity domain
model from instrument data flowing through the data lakes. The EDDT defines the
event-driven mechanisms through which the model is maintained and through which
consumers access it via formally defined Points-of-View.

### Domain

A bounded area of knowledge or responsibility. The term requires a qualifier
to be specific — for example, "data domain model" (the structured
representation of observed state within the EDDT), "observation domain" (the
bounded subject area observed by a class of instruments), or "transport
domain" (the network and protocol layers carrying observations). The data
domain model is the common subject of both snapshot and deltaflow
Points-of-View — snapshot and deltaflow are two views over the same domain,
differing only in whether they present current state or the stream of
transitions.

### Data Lake

The collective storage infrastructure that holds data across all tiers (Bronze,
Silver, Gold) and storage representations (columnar, relational). The data lake
is the persistent foundation of the Orient phase.

### Instrument

An outward-facing integration component purpose-built for a particular external
interface. An instrument collects observation data from a specific part of the
operator's observed infrastructure. Different instruments expose data through
different mechanisms (event-driven, polling, or packet monitoring).

### Telemetry

The inward-facing observation of the system's own health and behaviour.
Telemetry captures metrics, traces, and logs from the platform's deployed
components, distinct from the external observation performed by instruments.

### Schema

The formal definition of the structure, types, and constraints of data at a
given tier. At Bronze, schemas are dictated by the instrument. At Silver and
Gold, schemas conform to the system's normalised data domain model. Schemas are
versioned and evolve over time.

### Canonical Schema

The single, authoritative definition of a data structure at a given tier. A
canonical schema specifies field names, types, nesting, and ordering. All access
pattern representations of the same data must derive from the same canonical
schema, ensuring structural alignment across points-of-view. Schema evolution is
applied to the canonical schema; access pattern representations are regenerated
from it.

### Schema Projection

The expression of a canonical schema within a specific access pattern. The
subject projection defines the notification payload layout. The columnar
projection defines the column layout and encoding. The relational projection
defines the table and column definitions. Projections derived from the same
canonical schema are structurally aligned, enabling zero-copy transfer between
access patterns where the underlying representations support it.

### Data Lineage

The documented path that a piece of data took from its originating source
through transformations to its current storage representation. For example, a
PostgreSQL row may have arrived via direct event subject write (low-latency
path) or via materialisation from Parquet (batch path). Data lineage is a
component of data provenance metadata.

### Origin

The first point at which a piece of data entered the system — the source
instrument or producer that originally observed or minted it. Origin is the
head of a data item's data lineage; together, origin and lineage constitute its
data provenance.

### Digital Twin

A continuously reconstructed, high-fidelity model of observed state — in this
context, a model of the operator's observed environment derived from instrument
data flowing through the data lakes. The digital twin models only observed
state, not desired state. The Event-Driven Digital Twin (EDDT) extends this
concept by defining the event-driven mechanisms and Points-of-View through
which the twin is maintained and accessed.

### Materialisation

The process of producing a persistent storage representation from another
representation. Typically refers to populating PostgreSQL tables by reading from
Parquet files, producing effectively materialised views of the columnar data. An
alternative to direct event subject writes (low-latency path) and to virtual
access via DuckDB plugins (query-time path).

### Chain

The ordered sequence of deltas anchored by a snapshot that together describe
the evolution of a single domain entity. Every chain carries a unique
`ChainID` identifying its causal history, and a strictly-monotonic
`Sequence` numbering its constituent deltas — the anchoring Snapshot sits at
`Sequence = 0` and subsequent deltas increase monotonically. A consumer
subscribing to a chain reconstructs entity state by applying deltas in
Sequence order from the anchor. Chains are single-writer by design — at most
one producer holds ownership of a chain's Sequence space at a time — which
sidesteps the coordination cost of distributed writes and enables horizontal
scaling via shard-based chain distribution across producers and consumers.

### Taint

The set of Sequences skipped between a chain's anchor and a consumer's current
frontier, presumed missing and carried as consumer-side bookkeeping. Taint
arises when deltas are lost in transit or arrive outside an acceptable
reordering window. Under the design's gap-tolerant application rule, deltas
can still be applied across taint and the resulting snapshot is flagged as
partial for the missing Sequences. Taint can be cleared either by
late-arriving deltas that fill the gap (under forward-looking strong-handling
semantics on tagged fields) or by chain recovery that re-seeds the consumer
from a fresh snapshot.

### Observation Vocabulary

The representation mode in which records name discrete events — *"what
happened"* — rather than canonical state. Characteristic of Bronze-tier
records, where schemas are dictated by the observing instrument and
records typically correspond to protocol-level events. Observation-vocabulary
schemas are shaped by external authorities (standards bodies, vendor APIs)
and reflect *how the world is being observed*, not what the system concludes
from the observations. Contrast with **Fact Vocabulary**. See also
**Bronze Tier**.

### Fact Vocabulary

The representation mode in which records name canonical state —
*"what is true now"* (Snapshot) or *"what changed"* (Delta) — rather
than discrete events. Characteristic of Silver-tier and above records,
where schemas conform to a hand-authored domain model. Fact-vocabulary
schemas are authored internally by Systems Analysts and Data Engineers,
reflecting *what the system decides the observations mean*. The boundary
between observation vocabulary and fact vocabulary is the OODA
Observe→Orient handoff, and also the primary human-lifecycle role seam
at the Bronze↔Silver interface. Contrast with **Observation Vocabulary**.
See also **Silver Tier**, **Snapshot**, and **Deltaflow**.

### Conformance Level

The design commitment that tier designations (Bronze, Silver, Gold) reflect
the **degree of schema conformance and data quality** applied to a chain's
content, not the chain's topological position relative to instruments. A
direct-from-instrument stream and a composite stream emitted by a routing
aggregator can both legitimately be Bronze if neither commits to a canonical
schema; an aggregator that interprets and conforms its inputs against a
canonical schema produces Silver regardless of whether its inputs are
instrument streams or other Bronze chains. This framing decouples tier
semantics from pipeline topology and allows multi-source aggregation patterns
without inventing new tier categories.

### Frontier

A consumer-side progress marker tracking the highest `Sequence` a consumer
has applied for a given chain — the leading edge of applied work, in the
ordinary "high-water-mark" sense over per-chain `Sequence`. Together with
the consumer's **taint set** (the Sequences left unclaimed behind the
frontier), the frontier constitutes the consumer's complete bookkeeping
state for a chain under the gap-tolerant application rule. The frontier
provides three capabilities: idempotent delivery (deltas at or below the
frontier are duplicates and discarded), gap detection (a delta beyond
`frontier + 1` reveals a gap that contributes to taint), and subscription
resume (a reconnecting consumer requests "everything after my frontier").
The frontier is anchored per-chain rather than per-partition.

### Conflict-free Replicated Data Type (CRDT)

A class of data structures designed to support concurrent updates across
distributed replicas without requiring coordination. CRDTs guarantee that
any sequence of operations — regardless of order, duplication, or delivery
timing — converges to the same final state. The EDDT's forward-looking
strong-handling semantics (for late-arriving deltas on tagged fields) rely
on CRDT patterns to make out-of-order application safe by construction.
Key CRDT families referenced in the design include the Last-Writer-Wins
(LWW) Register for scalars and the Observed-Remove Set (ORSet) for
collections.

### Last-Writer-Wins Register (LWW-Register)

A CRDT for scalar fields that resolves concurrent or reordered assertions
by retaining the value from the assertion with the highest-priority
ordering key — typically a logical clock or, in the EDDT's case, the
chain's `Sequence`. A per-field LWW-Register with Sequence as the ordering
key is the mechanism by which scalar fields can safely apply out-of-order
deltas under strong-handling semantics: a late-arriving assertion with
lower Sequence than the currently-applied value is simply rejected.

### Observed-Remove Set (ORSet)

A CRDT for set-valued fields that supports commutative add-and-remove
semantics without loss of intended state under concurrent or reordered
operations. Each added element carries a unique identifier (typically the
Sequence at which it was added); removes target specific prior additions
rather than the element's logical identity. Final membership is computed
by resolving to the highest-Sequence claim per element. ORSet is the
pattern that would restore commutativity to set-based list operations
(`Added<Field>`, `Removed<Field>`) under forward-looking strong-handling
semantics in the EDDT.

---

## 4. Data Tiering

### Tier

A quality-defined layer in the data storage hierarchy. Data progresses through
tiers as it is normalised, correlated, deduplicated, enriched, and shaped for
consumption. The system uses three tiers: Bronze, Silver, and Gold. This tiered
structure is an instance of the medallion architecture, an industry-standard
pattern that separates concerns of durability (Bronze), quality (Silver), and
business semantics (Gold). In prose, "level" is sometimes used informally as a
synonym for tier when the formal medallion designation is unnecessary within the
context.

### Bronze Tier

The raw, immutable landing zone. Bronze stores records exactly as received from
ingestion, with no schema transformation beyond what is necessary for transport
or potential downsampling. Malformed or incomplete records are preserved. The
Bronze tier serves as the system's durable audit trail and the foundation for
retrospective reprocessing. The Bronze designation is defined by **conformance
level**, not topological placement — it denotes minimally-conformed input that
has not yet committed to a canonical schema. Bronze records carry an
**observation vocabulary** (events shaped by the observing instrument).
Direct-from-instrument streams are the archetypal Bronze source, but composite
or routing aggregators that merge or re-route records without committing to a
canonical schema also legitimately produce Bronze output.

### Silver Tier

The cleaned, conformed layer. Silver data has been validated, deduplicated, and
conformed to a consistent schema. It is suitable for analytical consumption
without requiring consumers to handle data quality concerns. Silver is the first
tier where relational modelling is appropriate. Silver records carry a
**fact vocabulary** (canonical Snapshot/Delta records against a hand-authored
domain model).

### Gold Tier

The business-aligned consumption layer. Gold data has been aggregated, enriched,
and structured to serve specific business use cases — dashboards, reports, ML
feature stores, or operational queries. Gold datasets are optimised for
particular consumption patterns.

---

## 5. Points-of-View

### Point-of-View (POV)

A formally defined interface through which consumers access the EDDT's model of
reality. Each POV is defined by the intersection of an access pattern and a data
nature. The system defines six POVs.

### Access Pattern

One of three fundamentally different mechanisms for consuming data, each serving
a distinct lifecycle stage: columnar (scan-oriented, for analytical exploration
and batch processing), relational (row-oriented, for operational queries and
application integration), and subject (push-based event delivery, for real-time
consumption).

### Columnar

An access pattern optimised for scan-oriented analytical workloads. Columnar
storage (e.g., Parquet) organises data by column rather than by row, enabling
efficient compression, column pruning, and predicate pushdown over large
datasets. Columnar access serves the "build" lifecycle stage — exploration,
training, and batch analysis.

### Relational

An access pattern optimised for row-oriented operational workloads. Relational
storage (e.g., PostgreSQL) organises data by row with support for joins,
transactions, and point lookups. Relational access serves the "operate"
lifecycle stage — serving APIs, interactive queries, dashboards, and calibration
interfaces.

### Subject

A named channel in the event-driven messaging system to which producers publish
and consumers subscribe. Subjects provide scoped, topic-based routing of
notifications. In the context of the EDDT, subjects are organised by tier
namespace (Bronze, Silver, Gold) and by data nature (snapshot, deltaflow).
Subject-based access serves the "react" lifecycle stage — real-time signal
generation, automated governance, and inter-service propagation.

### Tabular

A collective term encompassing both columnar and relational access patterns.
Used where the distinction between columnar and relational is not material to
the statement being made.

### Data Nature

One of two views over the same underlying domain model exposed through a POV:
snapshot (the current state at a point in time) or deltaflow (the history of
transitions that produced it). Both views are drawn from the same domain —
they differ only in whether they represent the state or the change stream. At
Bronze, the primary data nature depends on the instrument. At Silver and above,
deltaflow is generally the primary representation from which snapshot views
are derived.

### Snapshot

The state view of a Point-of-View — a point-in-time, self-contained
representation of the current state of the domain model. A snapshot is
self-anchoring and interpretable without reference to any prior state. As such,
a snapshot is the degenerate case of a delta: its causal chain has length zero.
Snapshot granularity matches deltaflow granularity at the same tier: a Silver
snapshot and a Silver deltaflow describe the same domain at the same fidelity,
differing only in whether they present the state or the transitions.

### Deltaflow

The transition view of a Point-of-View — the stream of changes (events,
mutations, insertions, deletions) that drive the evolution of the domain
model's state. Each delta describes a change at a point in time and must be
interpreted within its causal chain; recovering the current state of a domain
entity at a given level of granularity requires following that chain back to
a snapshot. At Bronze, deltaflow may be the primary data nature (for
event-driven instruments) or may need to be inferred retrospectively from
successive snapshots (for polling-based instruments).

### Delta

A representation of the change of a subject's state at a point in time. The
deltaflow is a stream of deltas.

### Subject Namespace

A tier-segregated naming structure that organises event subjects by quality
level (Bronze, Silver, Gold) and data nature (snapshot, deltaflow). Subject
namespaces physically isolate event streams of different tiers so that consumers
— such as real-time signal generators — subscribe only to data at the
appropriate quality level. In prose, "namespace" may be used alone when the
context is unambiguous, though care should be taken to avoid confusion with
infrastructure-level namespaces (e.g., Kubernetes namespaces).

---

## 6. Decide & Act

### Systems Analysis

The human-mediated investigation of data and system behaviour that spans the
left side of the V-model. Systems analysis encompasses the activities through
which humans explore data, form hypotheses, validate findings, and produce the
insights and requirements that inform new system capabilities. It may be
specialised by discipline — systems research, data science, engineering design —
depending on the lifecycle stage and the nature of the investigation. Systems
analysis operates across all tiers: Bronze data must be investigated to
understand instrument output and design transformation pipelines, while Silver
and Gold tier data are the primary consumption tiers for analytical findings and
hypothesis testing.

### Data Processing

The automated transformations, enrichments, and computations that execute within
the EDDT runtime pipeline without human initiation. Data processing spans the
Orient phase (tier promotion from Bronze through Silver to Gold) and the Decide
phase (signal generation, automated calibration). It operates continuously or on
schedule within the production environment.

### Analytics

An informal collective term covering both systems analysis (human-mediated) and
data processing (automated) activities. May be used in prose when the context
makes the intended sense unambiguous. Where precision is required, use "systems
analysis" or "data processing" instead.

### Algorithmics

The codification of systems analysis findings and capability requirements into
automated logic — models, rules, classifiers, or detection algorithms. This is a
human-in-the-loop engineering step: engineers and data scientists translate
validated insights and design requirements into deployable code that will
execute as part of the data processing pipeline. Algorithmics is the bridge
between human investigation (systems analysis) and automated execution (data
processing).

### Calibration

The tuning of algorithmic parameters against production data to maintain
accuracy as the observed environment evolves. Calibration has two distinct
modes: automated algorithmic calibration (using historical tabular data, handled
by the data processing pipeline) and engineer-mediated calibration (using
interactive operational views, handled by human operators as part of systems
analysis).

### Signal Generator

A real-time component of the data processing pipeline that consumes Silver tier
subject deltaflow notifications and produces signals — automated outputs that
indicate detected patterns, anomalies, or threshold breaches.

### Governor

An automated actuator in the Act phase. Governors receive signals and push
changes to interfacing systems without human intervention. Governors operate
within predefined policy boundaries and are subject to quality gates before
deployment.

### Operational Playbook

A structured procedure in the Act phase that guides human-mediated responses.
Playbooks define the steps, decision criteria, and escalation paths that field
and operations personnel follow when responding to signals or operational
conditions that require human judgement.

---

## 7. Lifecycle & Governance

### V-Model

A systems engineering lifecycle model comprising three phases: Concept &
Definition (left side) — defining needs, requirements, architecture, and design;
Realisation (bottom) — implementing system elements; and Integration &
Verification (right side) — assembling, verifying, and validating the complete
system.

### Verification

Confirmation that the system complies with its specified requirements — "are we
building the system right?" Verification is performed against technical
requirements and design specifications.

### Validation

Confirmation that the system, when deployed, accomplishes its intended use in
its operational environment — "are we building the right system?" Validation is
performed against stakeholder needs and operational expectations.

### Accretion

The principle that each completed lifecycle iteration adds permanently to the
source code and production deployments, forming an expanding operational
baseline. Accretion is governed, not accidental — capabilities must pass quality
gates before they are permitted to accrete into production.

### Recursive Iteration

When a lifecycle iteration stalls for lack of empirical data, it spawns a
subordinate iteration to build the enabling infrastructure. The subordinate
system remains in production permanently, becoming part of the accreted
baseline.

### Capability Maturation

The continuous, iterative progression of analytical capability — from
exploratory analysis through formalised algorithmics, calibration, and real-time
signal generation. This progression is driven by both automated subsystems that
adapt within operational cycles and human-mediated engineering processes that
advance capabilities across lifecycle iterations.

### Baseline

A stable, verified, and configuration-managed snapshot of the system's source
code, data models, and production deployments at a point in time. Each governed
accretion produces a new baseline. Baselines are the unit of configuration
management across all deployments.

### Data Provenance

Metadata that records *where data came from and how it was derived* — its
origin (the source instrument or producer that first observed or minted the
data) together with its data lineage (the path through components and
transformations to the current representation). Provenance is a *where-from*
record and is distinct from data quality (completeness, fidelity), which is
carried separately as taint / quality signals; quality assessment may draw on
provenance for attribution. Provenance is preserved alongside the data at each
tier ([N-EDDT-012][n-eddt-012]) so that any stored or reconstructed state can be explained,
audited, and traced back to its origins.

### Eventual Consistency

The system property whereby different representations and tiers of the same
underlying data may temporarily diverge, converging over time as asynchronous
processing completes. Given the inherently asynchronous nature of observation,
eventual consistency is accepted across representations and tiers rather than
requiring strict cross-POV synchronisation.

### Traceability

The ability to link each requirement forward to the system elements that
implement it, and backward to the parent needs or requirements from which it was
derived. Traceability ensures that every requirement can be justified, every
need is addressed, and changes propagate correctly through the requirements
hierarchy. A requirement expression (statement plus attributes) is the unit of
traceability.

### Quality Gate

A verification checkpoint that a capability must pass before it is permitted to
accrete into the production runtime environment. Quality gates enforce that
capabilities meet defined verification and validation criteria before they
become part of the operational baseline.

### Stakeholder

A person, group, or organisation with an interest in the system or its
lifecycle. Stakeholders include those who specify needs, develop the system,
operate it, maintain it, and are affected by it.

---
